title: The Hexagon Chamber

features.name: the-wax-architecture
features.desc-on-touch: Honeycomb cells rise before you, but larger than any bee could build—each hexagonal chamber holds a single file, a single memory. The bees and the archive agreed on this shape long ago.

features.name: the-cataloguing-dance
features.desc-on-touch: A figure traces patterns between the cells—perhaps a beekeeper, perhaps the archive itself—moving in the waggle-dance of navigation but marking archives, not flowers.

features.name: the-light-that-bees-see
features.desc-on-touch: A pool of luminescence—the ultraviolet patterns that guide bees to pollen—but if you look into it, you see archive indices, cross-references, knowledge mapped in the spectrum bees use to read.

features.name: the-beekeeper-s-notation
features.desc-on-touch: Marks on the chamber wall: wax impressions, but also handwriting, dates, recipe notes. The beekeeper was recording not just hive health but the history of summer itself.

features.name: the-hexagon-that-doesn-t-close
features.desc-on-touch: One cell in the wall is incomplete—one wall missing—and you realise the whole chamber is one cell, built around you, and you are still inside.

inscription: the bees learned to number the days by remembering the flowers; the archive learned to number the days by remembering what the bees said

file.name: Summer_1954_Hive_Records_and_Provisional_Archive_Inventory.mp3
file.note: status: germinating—audio contains both bee-dance audio and human voice cataloguing the same moments; neither has temporal priority

exits.label: back to the clearing, where the bees still tend their library
toward the deeper archive, where all the hexagons connect into one vast memory
down into the soil where hexagons appear in crystal and quartz
